text: Last class you said: Shall we start with the agenda?
Now we ask: How do you walk an audience through your slides, step by step?

Good morning. Today I would like to give you a quick overview of our compliance project, in three parts.
Thank you, Doctor. Please walk us through the first slide.
First, let us look at the results. On this slide there is one key figure: a forty percent increase in completed training.
That is a strong result. And what comes after the results?
Next, let us move on to the budget and timeline. There are two charts here, and I will highlight the most important one.
Finally, to sum up: the project is on track and on budget. Now I am happy to take any questions.
Today I would like to give you a quick overview.
Next, let us move on to the next slide.

To sum up, I am happy to take any questions.

Hello Doctor Nilo, this is Sofia from the FIFA Leadership in Football program office. Next week you will give a short presentation to the committee about the Corinthians compliance project, so here is some friendly advice on how to structure it. A good presentation has three parts: an introduction, a body, and a conclusion. In the introduction, give a quick overview of your topic and tell the audience what you will cover. For example, you can say, today I would like to give you an overview of our compliance results. Then, in the body, signpost every step so people can follow you. Use words like first, next, then, and finally to move from one slide to the next. When you reach an important number, point it out clearly, for example, as you can see on this slide, there is a strong increase in completed training. Keep each slide simple, with one key point and one figure. At the end, in your conclusion, sum up your main points and invite questions. You can say, to sum up, our compliance is on track, and now I am happy to take any questions. Remember to face the audience and not the slide, and speak slowly. You have prepared this project for months, Doctor, so just walk us through it with confidence. Good luck.

Let me tell you about the presentation I gave last week to the compliance committee. It was my first full presentation in English, and I will be honest, my hands were a little cold before I started. I opened with a short introduction and gave the audience an overview of the three topics I would cover. Then I moved through the body slide by slide. To keep everyone with me, I used signposts all the time. I would say, first, let us look at the results, then I would say, next, I will explain the budget, and after that, let us move on to the timeline. On one slide there was an important figure, a forty percent increase in completed training, so I pointed it out and paused for a moment so the audience could read it. One person did not follow the data, so I went back one slide and clarified it before I continued. Near the end, I reached my conclusion and used a clear summary. To sum up, I said, the project is on track and on budget. Finally, I invited questions and answered three of them. I remembered Sofia's advice and faced the committee instead of the screen. It was not perfect, but for the first time I felt that the audience was following every step of my presentation.

Let me give you a quick overview of the project.

Here are the steps of a presentation. First, give the audience a short overview of your topic. Then walk them through the body, one slide at a time. After that, point out the key figure and highlight it. Next, move on to the last slide. Finally, sum up your main points and invite questions.
Today I would like to give you a quick overview.
Next, let us move on to the next slide.
To sum up, I am happy to take any questions.
